title: Chapter 31: The Testimony
content: The kitchen is warm in a way the rest of Bly Manor has never been.

I sit at the scarred wooden table where the staff has eaten for a century, where Mrs. Grose has kneaded bread and peeled potatoes and held this house together with her hands, and the warmth is not from the oven — though the oven is on, though there's flour on her forearms and a smear of it across her jaw — but from thirty years of standing in this exact spot. The walls hold it. The copper pans hanging from their hooks hold it. Even the air holds it, thick and close, smelling of yeast and dried herbs and something sweeter underneath, something like the memory of every meal she's ever made for children who are not her own.

She chose the kitchen. Not the sitting room where I've been conducting my interviews, where the chairs are arranged just so and the light is good for taking notes. She chose her territory. And I understand why: because what she's about to tell me will unmake her, and she wants to be unmade in the place where she has been most herself.

There are two cups of tea between us. Mine has gone cold. I drink it anyway — the bitter, tannic taste of it grounding me, keeping me present in my body while my mind races ahead to what's coming. Mrs. Grose hasn't touched hers. She stands at the counter instead of sitting, her back half-turned to me, working a ball of dough with hands that know this motion the way my lungs know breathing. Automatic. Something to do while the words arrange themselves behind her teeth.
"I'm telling you before I tell the court," she says, not looking at me. "I need to say it once. To someone who'll understand what it means."
"Alright."
"That night. The night Miss Jessel —" She stops. The dough receives the pressure of her palms. "I was awake."
I say nothing. The refrigerator hums. Outside, the wind moves through the rhododendrons and the sound is like breathing.

"I couldn't sleep. I never could sleep well in that house. Not since — well. Not for a long time. I heard the twins. Their doors. The stairs." She turns the dough, presses it flat, folds it. "I heard them go out."

She still won't look at me. I watch her hands. The flour dust rises in the lamplight, catching the yellow glow, and for a moment it looks like snow, like ash, like something that should mean more than it does.
"I followed them. Down the corridor, through the garden door. Across the lawn." A long pause. Her hands slow. "To the lake."

The word lands in the warm kitchen air and changes it. Lake. That lake. The one I've circled on maps and measured in photographs and dreamed about in versions I can't keep straight. The one that took Jessel. The one that held her.
"I saw her," Mrs. Grose says. "Miss Jessel. She was already at the water. She was — she was taking off her shoes."

I grip the cold cup. The ceramic is slick with condensation. The tea inside has separated, a thin film of cream floating on the surface, and I think: this is what it feels like to hear the truth. Everything separates. The layers show themselves.

"Miles was standing on the shore," she continues, and her voice has gone flat now, recitative, like she's reading from a deposition. Maybe she is. Maybe she's been rehearsing this for weeks, for months, for the entire time I've been at Bly, practicing the words in this kitchen while the bread rose and the soup simmered and no one asked her what was wrong. "He had his phone. He was — he was holding it up. Filming."
The dough rests. She doesn't touch it.

"Flora was behind him. She had her hand on his arm. Pulling. You know how she does — that little tug, like she's trying to reel him back from the edge of something. And Miles pushed her away. Pushed her. She stumbled. She didn't fall, but she stumbled, and he didn't even look at her. He kept the phone pointed at the water."

Mrs. Grose finally turns. Her eyes are dry. That's the worst part. Not tears — I could bear tears. The dryness is worse. It means she's been here before, inside this memory, has walked through it so many times the path is worn smooth.

"Miss Jessel walked into the water," she says. "She didn't hesitate. She didn't look back. She just — walked in. Like she was walking into a room. Like it was somewhere she'd been before and knew the way."
I taste the cold tea in my throat, bitter and stale. The oven ticks as it heats. Somewhere in the walls, a pipe groans.
"And I stood there," Mrs. Grose says. "Behind the garden door. I watched. I watched a woman walk into the water and a boy film it and a girl try to stop it and fail. And I did nothing."

The silence that follows is not empty. It is full. Full of thirty years of being the one who keeps everything together, thirty years of seeing things and saying nothing because that was the job, because that was the shape of her life at Bly, because Peter Quint built a house where silence was the rent you paid for the roof over your head.

"I didn't find her room empty in the morning," Mrs. Grose says, and now she moves to the table, pulls out the chair across from me, sits. Her flour-dusted hands rest on the wood and leave pale prints. "That's what I told everyone. That's what I told myself. But it wasn't true. I knew she wasn't coming back. I knew before I went to her room that she was gone."
She looks at me. Her eyes are brown, dark, and deep in the way of people who have spent their lives watching from the edges of rooms.

"I packed her suitcase myself. Half-packed. I didn't know what to keep and what to throw away. It didn't matter — no one was coming for it. No one ever came for her things." Her mouth twists. "I sat on her bed and I held her hairbrush and I could smell her shampoo and I thought: I should have screamed. I should have woken the house. I should have run down to the water and pulled her out. But I didn't. I stood behind that door and I watched and I let the door close and I went back to bed."

I think of the annotations. Jessel's handwriting in the margins of Peter's book, the careful, desperate notes that said: *The Game can't be won. Don't trust the trust. The architecture is the cage.* I think of how I read them as evidence, as data, as pieces of the puzzle I was building. Not as a woman's last attempt to leave a record of the truth.
"She knew," I say. "Jessel knew the Game was unwinnable."
"Yes."
"She knew the trust was designed. That it was never real."

"Peter designed everything," Mrs. Grose says. "The trust. The roles. The way the children —" She stops herself. Breathes. "He designed every exit and sealed every exit and the only one he never thought to design for was death. That was the one door he couldn't lock. So she took it."

The warmth of the kitchen presses against me. The smell of the dough, the hum of the refrigerator, the faint char from the oven where a previous batch left its mark. All of it so ordinary. All of it so deliberately ordinary, a woman making bread while she confesses to the worst thing she's ever done, because ordinary is the only frame that can hold this much weight.

"I could have stopped it," Mrs. Grose says, and now the flatness cracks, now the recitative falters, now her voice is just a voice, old and tired and shaking with the effort of laying itself bare. "I could have screamed. I could have woken the house. But I was so tired, Maeve. I was so tired of being the one who keeps everything together. And for one minute — just one minute — I wanted someone else to be responsible. So I stood there. And I watched. And I let it happen."

The flour on her hands has dried now, cracking along the lines of her palms. I look at those hands. Hands that cooked and cleaned and held and soothed and opened doors and closed them. Hands that packed a dead woman's suitcase. Hands that stayed at her sides while a woman walked into black water.

I should feel something. I know I should feel something. There should be horror, or pity, or the particular fury that comes from hearing about preventable tragedy. These are the expected responses. This is what a good person would feel.
What I feel is: *Finally. A witness.*
"You're telling the court," I say.

It's not a question. It's not absolution. It's the only thing that matters, and I am aware, in some distant and dispassionate way, that my response is wrong. That the correct answer to a confession like Mrs. Grose's is not a logistical observation. That a woman who has just admitted to standing by while another woman drowned deserves more than a statement about legal proceedings.
But morality is a luxury. Justice requires witnesses.
"I know what I did," Mrs. Grose says quietly. "I've carried it for —"

"I'm not asking you to carry it differently. I'm not asking you to feel worse. I'm telling you that your guilt is not useful to anyone. But your testimony is." I meet her eyes. "You're telling the court. That's what matters now."
She stares at me. I can see her recalibrating, adjusting her expectations of what kind of person I am. She expected judgment or mercy. I've given her neither. I've given her a task.
"Will it matter?" she asks. "The court. Will it change anything?"

"I don't know," I say. "But there's no version of this that gets better without your account on record. The injunction preserved the footage. Your testimony gives it context. Miles's phone, pointed at the water — that's evidence of participation. Flora trying to pull him back — that's evidence of coercion. Jessel walking in — that's the outcome of Peter's design. You saw it. You were there. The court needs to hear it from someone who was there."
I'm laying it out like a strategy. Like a brief. Like a Game move. I know this. I hear myself and I think: *You sound like him.* And I don't stop.
Mrs. Grose's hands are flat on the table. The flour prints she left have begun to fade, the moisture in the wood absorbing them. Soon there will be nothing there at all.
"Maeve," she says.
"Yes."
"Are you Peter's daughter?"

The question should hit differently than it does. It should feel like an accusation or an unmasking. Instead it feels like the natural endpoint of this conversation — two women in a kitchen, the truth finally told, the only question left being the one that's been circling me since I arrived at Bly, since I opened Peter's book, since I first understood what he built and recognized the architecture of my own mind inside it.
"Yes."
Mrs. Grose nods. Slowly. Like she confirmed something she already knew.

"Then you have his eyes," she says. "I see it now. The way you take everything in. The way you — you file it. You catalogue it. You never waste a piece of information. That's what he did. That's how he built the Game. He watched and he understood and he used what he understood."
"I'm not —"

"I'm not finished." Her voice carries an authority I haven't heard from her before. Not the soft competence of the housekeeper, not the careful evasion of someone with secrets. This is the voice of a woman who has decided to speak and will not be interrupted. "You have his capacity for the long game. I see it. You've been playing one since you walked through that door. Everything you've done — every question, every document, every conversation — it's all in service of something. Something you haven't told me. Something you might not have told yourself."
The oven clicks off. The kitchen is very quiet.
"Don't become him, Maeve."
I wait.

"The Game needs to end," she says. "Not be won. There's a difference, and it matters, and if you can't see it, then you're already lost. Peter thought he could win. That was his mistake. He thought control was the same as victory, and he built a machine that proved him wrong, and people died because of it." She leans forward. "You can end this. You have the testimony, the evidence, the footage. You can bring it all down. But if you try to win — if you try to use what you've gathered to play one last move, to prove something, to demonstrate that you're smarter than he was — then you're just playing his Game with his rules, and the Game always wins. It always wins, Maeve. It won, and Peter's dead, and Miss Jessel's dead, and those children are —"
She stops. The word *ruined* hangs in the air, unspoken but present, as real as the flour on the table, as the cold tea in my cup.
"I know," I say.

And I do know. I understand, with the same clarity that lets me see the structure of Peter's design, that there is a difference between ending the Game and winning it. I understand that Mrs. Grose is right. I understand that the testimony she'll give is not a move in a strategy but a fact that stands outside strategy, a fact that belongs to the world and not to the Game.
I understand all of this.
I nod.

But I'm not sure I know the difference anymore. Not in my hands. Not in the part of me that's been cataloguing and filing and arranging since I walked through Bly's front door. Not in the part that heard Mrs. Grose's confession and thought *witness* before it thought *grief.* The difference between ending and winning — I can articulate it, I can defend it, I can write it in the margins of Peter's book alongside Jessel's annotations. But I can't feel it. The place where that distinction should live, the part of me that should recoil from the word *win* the way Jessel's hand must have recoiled from the water, is quiet.
It's so quiet.

Mrs. Grose rises. She returns to the counter, to the dough, to the motion that has carried her through thirty years of seeing too much and saying too little. Her hands work the bread. The flour rises around her. The kitchen holds her.

I sit with my cold tea and my catalogue of evidence and my father's eyes, and I think about exits — the ones Peter designed, the ones he didn't, the one Jessel took, the one Mrs. Grose refused, the one I'm still looking for. And I wonder whether the exit I find will lead me out of the Game or deeper into its architecture, whether ending and winning are truly different or just two words for the same door, whether I'm the one who closes it or the one who walks through.
The dough yields under Mrs. Grose's hands. It has no choice. That's what dough does. It takes the shape you give it.

I stand. I leave the kitchen. I walk through the corridor where Mrs. Grose stood behind a door and watched a woman die, and I don't look at the garden, and I don't look at the lake, and I carry the testimony like a stone.
It's the heaviest thing I've ever held. And I'm not sure I'm carrying it toward justice or toward the next move.
I'm not sure I can tell the difference.
